meta.title: 9.8 Checkpoint
meta.description: Analyze a distributed system design for consistency, availability, and partition tolerance tradeoffs.

blocks.content:
## Level 9 Checkpoint

Apply distributed systems theory to real design problems.

blocks.question: You are designing a global shopping cart for an e-commerce site. Users can add items from any region. The cart must be available even during network partitions between regions. Do you choose CP or AP? What consistency model do you use?
blocks.answer: **Choose AP (available + partition tolerant).** A shopping cart must always be available — users should always be able to add items. During a network partition, each region accepts writes to its local cart replica. When the partition heals, merge the carts. Use a **CRDT (G-Set or OR-Set)** for the cart — add operations are commutative and merge naturally. Conflicts (same item added in two regions) resolve to 'item is in cart' (union). This is exactly how Amazon's Dynamo-based shopping cart works. The tradeoff: during a partition, a user might see a slightly stale cart if they switch regions. But they never lose items, and the cart is always available.

blocks.title: Design a distributed lock service (like ZooKeeper or etcd). It must guarantee mutual exclusion — only one client holds the lock at a time. Is this a CP or AP system? What consensus algorithm would you use? What happens during a network partition?
blocks.solution: **CP system.** Mutual exclusion requires strong consistency — you cannot have two clients thinking they hold the lock. Use **Raft consensus** for the lock service cluster (3 or 5 nodes). The Raft leader processes lock requests. A lock is granted only when a majority of nodes agree. During a network partition: the partition with the majority continues granting locks. The minority partition cannot grant locks (it cannot reach consensus). Clients connected to the minority side see the lock service as unavailable. This is the correct behavior — availability is sacrificed to prevent two clients holding the same lock. This is exactly how etcd (used by Kubernetes) and ZooKeeper work.

blocks.content:
## Ready for Level 10?

If you can reason about CAP tradeoffs, choose appropriate consistency models, and apply distributed algorithms to real problems, you are ready. Level 10 covers the resilience layer — making systems survive the failures that distributed computing guarantees.
